en.extract:
The rufous casiornis (Casiornis rufus) is a species of bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is found in Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, Peru, Uruguay, and as a vagrant to Chile.


Taxonomy and systematics
The rufous casiornis was originally described as Thamnophilus rufus, mistakenly placing it in the "typical antbird" family Thamnophilidae. It was later moved to genus Casiornis that was erected in 1856. During the first half of the twentieth century many authors placed that genus in family Cotingidae but starting in the 1970s it was recognized as belonging to Tyrannidae. The rufous casiornis shares the genus with the ash-throated casiornis (C. fuscus) and the two form a superspecies. For a time they were treated as conspecific but early in the twenty-first century they were firmly established as separate species.
The rufous casiornis is monotypic.


Description
The rufous casiornis is 17 to 18 cm (6.7 to 7.1 in) long and weighs 22 to 27 g (0.78 to 0.95 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a rufous head, upperparts, wings, and tail. Their throat and breast are cinnamon with a whitish middle of the throat. Their belly is pale buffy yellowish. They have a dark brown iris, a dark bill with a pinkish base, and blackish gray legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The rufous casiornis is found from southeastern Peru south through much of Bolivia and Paraguay, into northern Argentina as far as Tucumán and Corrientes provinces, and slightly into northern Uruguay. Its range extends east into Brazil with the boundary roughly formed by central Rondônia, central Pará, western Piauí, southern Minas Gerais, and southern Mato Grosso do Sul. It has also occurred as a vagrant in Chile.
The rufous casiornis inhabits a variety of wooded landscapes including deciduous woodlands, gallery woodlands, wooded cerrado, and scrubby and wooded Chaco. It has been reported in dry forest in parts of Peru. In Brazil it ranges in elevation from near sea level to 1,500 m (4,900 ft). It locally reaches 2,500 m (8,200 ft) in Bolivia.


Behavior


Movement
The rufous casiornis is migratory but the timing and extent of its movements are known only in outline. It apparently is found in the northern half of its range only in the austral winter, though whether it entirely or partially leaves the southern half is unknown. It is considered as a non-breeding migrant in Peru.


Feeding
The rufous casiornis is thought to capture insect prey with sallies from a perch. Nothing else is known about its diet or feeding behavior.


Breeding
Two rufous casiornis nests are known. They were in tree cavities about 1.2 and 1.5 m (4 and 5 ft) above the ground. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.


Vocalization
The rufous casiornis is rather quiet. It makes a "very high, downslurred feeeèh" and a "brief, weak psee note".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the rufous casiornis as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is unknown and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered fairly common overall though rare in Peru. It is known in Uruguay only from 1999. It is found in protected areas in both its breeding and non-breeding ranges. However, "[m]uch of this species' preferred habitat has been converted for agricultural use and eucalypt (Eucalyptus) and pine (Pinus) plantations".


References

The '''rufous casiornis''' ('''''Casiornis rufus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Tyrannidae]], the tyrant flycatchers.<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=3 March 2025 }}</ref> It is found in [[Argentina]], [[Bolivia]], [[Brazil]], [[Paraguay]], [[Peru]], [[Uruguay]], and as a [[Vagrancy (biology)|vagrant]] to [[Chile]].<ref name=SACCcountries>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 30 March 2025. Species Lists of Birds for South American Countries and Territories. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCCountryLists.htm retrieved 30 March 2025</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The rufous casiornis was [[species description|originally described]] as ''Thamnophilus rufus'', mistakenly placing it in the "typical antbird" family [[Thamnophilidae]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Une Société de Naturalistes et d'Agriculteurs | date= 1816 | title=Nouveau dictionnaire d'histoire naturelle, appliquée aux arts, à l'agriculture, à l'économie rurale et domestique, à la médecine, etc. | publisher=Chez Deterville | volume=III |page=316 |language=French | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/21109452 |access-date=July 18, 2025 }}</ref> It was later moved to genus ''Casiornis'' that was erected in 1856.<ref name=IOC15.1/> During the first half of the twentieth century many authors placed that genus in family [[Cotingidae]] but starting in the 1970s it was recognized as belonging to [[Tyrannidae]].<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 30 March 2025. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved 30 March 2025</ref> The rufous casiornis shares the genus with the [[ash-throated casiornis]] (''C. fuscus'') and the two form a [[superspecies]]. For a time they were treated as [[conspecific]] but early in the twenty-first century they were firmly established as separate species.<ref name=SACClist/>

The rufous casiornis is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC15.1/>

==Description==

The rufous casiornis is {{convert|17|to|18|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|22|to|27|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a rufous head, upperparts, wings, and tail. Their throat and breast are cinnamon with a whitish middle of the throat. Their belly is pale buffy yellowish. They have a dark brown iris, a dark bill with a pinkish base, and blackish gray legs and feet.<ref name=RUCA-BOW>Scholes, E. (2020). Rufous Casiornis (''Casiornis rufus''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.rufcas2.01 retrieved July 18, 2025</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The rufous casiornis is found from southeastern Peru south through much of Bolivia and Paraguay, into northern Argentina as far as [[Tucumán Province|Tucumán]] and [[Corrientes Province|Corrientes]] provinces, and slightly into northern Uruguay.<ref name=RUCA-BOW/> Its range extends east into Brazil with the boundary roughly formed by central [[Rondônia]], central [[Pará]], western [[Piauí]], southern [[Minas Gerais]], and southern [[Mato Grosso do Sul]].<ref name=vanPerlo>{{cite book | last =van Perlo | first = Ber| title =A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil | publisher =Oxford University Press | date =2009 | location =New York | pages =328–329 | isbn =978-0-19-530155-7 }}</ref> It has also occurred as a vagrant in Chile.<ref name=SACCcountries/>

The rufous casiornis inhabits a variety of wooded landscapes including deciduous woodlands, [[gallery woodland]]s, wooded ''[[cerrado]]'', and scrubby and wooded [[Humid Chaco|Chaco]].<ref name=RUCA-BOW/> It has been reported in dry forest in parts of Peru.<ref name=Schulenberg>{{cite book | last =Schulenberg | first =T.S. | last2 =Stotz | first2 =D.F. | last3 =Lane | first3 =D.F. | last4 =O'Neill | first4 =J.P. | last5 =Parker | first5 =T.A. III | title =Birds of Peru | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =revised and updated |series=Princeton Field Guides | date =2010 | location =Princeton, NJ | page =478  |isbn = 978-0-691-13023-1 }}</ref> In Brazil it ranges in elevation from near sea level to {{convert|1500|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=vanPerlo/> It locally reaches {{convert|2500|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Bolivia.<ref name=RUCA-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The rufous casiornis is migratory but the timing and extent of its movements are known only in outline. It apparently is found in the northern half of its range only in the [[South|austral]] winter, though whether it entirely or partially leaves the southern half is unknown.<ref name=RUCA-BOW/> It is considered as a non-breeding migrant in Peru.<ref name=SACCcountries/><ref name=Schulenberg/>

===Feeding===

The rufous casiornis is thought to capture insect prey with sallies from a perch. Nothing else is known about its diet or feeding behavior.<ref name=RUCA-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Two rufous casiornis nests are known. They were in tree cavities about {{convert|1.2|and|1.5|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} above the ground. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.<ref name=RUCA-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Casiornis-rufus |species=the rufous casiornis}}

===Vocalization===

The rufous casiornis is rather quiet. It makes a "very high, downslurred ''feeeèh''"<ref name=vanPerlo/> and a "brief, weak ''psee'' note".<ref name=RUCA-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the rufous casiornis as being of Least Concern. It has a large range; its population size is unknown and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered fairly common overall<ref name=RUCA-BOW/> though rare in Peru.<ref name=Schulenberg/> It is known in Uruguay only from 1999.<ref name=Claramunt>{{cite journal|last1=Claramunt|first1=Santiago|last2=González|first2=Enrique M.|title=''Elaenia spectabilis'' y ''Casiornis rufa'', dos Tyrannidae nuevos para Uruguay (Aves: Passeriformes).|journal= Comunicaciones Zoológicas del Museo de Historia Natural de Montevideo|date=1999|volume=12|issue=194|pages=1–8|url=http://www.mna.gub.uy/innovaportal/file/12406/1/cz194.pdf}}</ref> It is found in protected areas in both its breeding and non-breeding ranges. However, "[m]uch of this species' preferred habitat has been converted for agricultural use and eucalypt (''[[Eucalyptus]]'') and pine (''[[Pinus]]'') plantations".<ref name=RUCA-BOW/>
